SYLLABUS: DESCRIPTION

This course provides a broad overview of computer science. It covers the evolution of computing, basic hardware and software components, data representation, binary systems, and the fundamental role of computers in modern society.

This course focuses on algorithmic problem-solving and foundational programming concepts. It covers basic syntax, data types, control structures, and functions using a high-level programming language to build simple applications.

This course explores the mathematical foundations necessary for computer science. It covers set theory, propositional and predicate logic, Boolean algebra, relations, functions, combinatorics, and introductory graph theory, providing the logical groundwork for algorithm design.

This course introduces complex programming paradigms and problem-solving techniques. It covers advanced data types, memory management, file handling, basic error handling, and an introduction to modular programming.

This course explores how data is organized and manipulated efficiently. It covers implementation and analysis of core structures like arrays, linked lists, stacks, queues, trees, and graphs, alongside fundamental searching and sorting algorithms.

This course examines the design, evaluation, and implementation of interactive computing systems for human use. It focuses on user-centered design principles, usability testing, accessibility, and the psychological aspects of how users interact with technology.

This course introduces the core concepts of database systems and data modeling. It covers Entity-Relationship (ER) modeling, relational database design, normalization, and the use of Structured Query Language (SQL) for data retrieval and manipulation.

This course details the fundamentals of digital logic design. It covers logic gates, Boolean algebra, combinational and sequential logic circuits, flip-flops, and the basic architectural building blocks of a computer's central processing unit.

This course dives into the Object-Oriented paradigm. It covers software design using classes and objects, core principles such as encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism, abstraction, and introductory software design patterns.

This course focuses on the end-to-end process of building software applications. It covers web, mobile, or desktop development, emphasizing user interfaces, back-end integration, and deploying functional software.
This course explores the algorithmic design techniques and mathematical analysis. It covers time and space complexity of algorithms using Big-O notation and explore the concepts of P and NP classes.

This course bridges the gap between hardware and software by examining how computer systems are structured. It covers instruction set architectures, CPU design, memory hierarchies, pipelining, and parallel processing.

This course that studies abstract machines and computation limits. It covers regular languages, finite state automata, context-free grammars, pushdown automata, and Turing machines, forming the basis for language processing and compiler design.

This course focuses on methodologies used to build large, complex software systems. It covers software development life cycles, requirements engineering, software architecture, testing, quality assurance, and project management.

This course introduces the concept of Artificial Intelligence (AI). It covers fundamental AI concepts, including search algorithms, knowledge representation, logic programming, expert systems, and a foundational overview of machine learning and neural networks.

This course explores the principles and practices of translating high-level programming languages into machine code. It covers lexical analysis, syntax analysis, semantic analysis, intermediate code generation, and code optimization techniques.

This course focuses on using advanced computing capabilities to understand and solve complex physical problems. It covers numerical methods, mathematical modeling, and algorithmic simulations used in scientific and engineering research.

This course equips students with the skills needed to conduct academic and technical research. It covers defining research problems, conducting literature reviews, understanding research methodologies, and formatting scientific papers.

This course is an industry-focused internship or applied project experience. Students are placed in a professional environment to apply their academic knowledge to real-world problems, gaining practical skills and workplace experience.

This course is the first phase of the capstone project. Students are expected to identify a significant problem in computer science, conduct a thorough literature review, propose a methodological solution, and successfully defend their research proposal.

This course studies computer networking concepts. It covers the OSI and TCP/IP models, network topologies, routing and switching protocols, local and wide area networks (LAN/WAN), and basic network security principles.
This course tailors to current industry trends or specific domains within computer science.

This course is the final phase of the capstone project. Building on Thesis 1, students will implement their proposed system or algorithm, conduct rigorous testing and data analysis, write the final research manuscript, and present a final oral defense.

This course explores the ethical, legal, and social implications of computing. It covers data privacy, intellectual property, cybercrime, the impact of technology on society, and professional codes of ethics for software engineers.

This course introduces the mathematical and algorithmic foundations of computer graphics. Students will learn about 2D and 3D rendering pipelines, geometric transformations, clipping, illumination models, shading, and the basics of computer animation.
